content:
# Multi-Keyframe LUT POC Results (2026-04-01)

## Approach
Pool pixel pairs from all 3 keyframes (1.77M total) to build a 33^3 base LUT + multi-frame LAB compensation + per-frame depth grading.

## Coverage Improvement
- Single-keyframe: 2,206/35,937 cells populated (6.1%)
- Multi-keyframe: 2,950/35,937 cells populated (8.2%) — 1.3x more

## Results

| Metric | Multi vs Full (avg delta) | Single vs Full (avg delta) |
|--------|--------------------------|---------------------------|
| R/G | **-0.002** | -0.005 |
| B/G | +0.005 | -0.025 |
| Saturation | **-0.002** | -0.026 |
| Hue | +3.7° | -3.6° |

Multi-keyframe substantially improved saturation matching (gap 0.002 vs 0.026) and R/G matching (gap 0.002 vs 0.005). B/G also much closer.

## Remaining issue
Hue still shifts by 3-12° depending on frame. The hue shift direction flipped — single was -3.6° (too yellow), multi is +3.7° (slightly too blue on some frames). This is the fundamental limit of a global LUT trying to approximate a spatially-aware neural network.

## Key insight
Multi-keyframe blending is clearly better than single-keyframe for color metrics, but the hue shift persists because the LUT can only capture color→color mappings, not the spatial context RAUNE-Net uses. The hue variation across frames comes from RAUNE-Net applying different corrections to the same color depending on its spatial neighborhood.
